{"covered_dims": {"R1.2": 0.7, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.5": 0.8, "R1.6": 0.95, "R1.7": 0.4}}

Summary: Rewrote the section to fix run-on sentences and tense inconsistencies, tightened the prose, surfaced missing methodological details, and flagged claims that need citation or additional evidence.

Key issues identified

1. Pervasive run-on sentences. Nearly every sentence chains three or four independent clauses with "and." This obscures the methodology. The section needs to be broken into shorter declarative sentences and reorganized into clear sub-blocks (protocol, sampling, results).

2. Tense drift. "We recruited," "are screened," "were admitted," "are receiving," "received," "show," "was" — the section oscillates between past, present, and present-progressive. Methods and results should be in consistent past tense.

3. Missing methodological detail that reviewers will ask for:
   - The five-point Likert anchors are not defined for any of the three dimensions.
   - "Crowdsourcing platform" is unnamed; for medical raters this matters (Prolific? a domain-specific service? institutional recruitment?). IRB/ethics approval is not mentioned.
   - The calibration task is described as "brief" with no detail on stimuli, pass criterion, or how many candidates were excluded.
   - Rater compensation, blinding procedures beyond order randomization, and whether raters could see prior cases are not stated.
   - It is unclear whether the 28 raters each saw all 500 cases or whether cases were distributed; with "three independent reviewers per pair," the assignment scheme matters.
   - Mean aggregation across three reviewers is reported but no dispersion (SD, CI) is given for the headline numbers.

4. Statistical reporting gaps:
   - No significance test or confidence interval accompanies the 3.8 vs 4.2 factual-accuracy gap. The reader cannot tell if the difference is meaningful.
   - "Clinical utility ratings followed a similar pattern" gives no numbers.
   - "Essentially no gap" on fluency is vague — give the means.
   - Krippendorff's alpha is reported as a range (0.41–0.58) without per-dimension values. Report each dimension separately. Also specify the metric variant (nominal/ordinal/interval) — for Likert data, ordinal alpha is standard.
   - "Moderate" agreement at α = 0.41 is generous; conventional thresholds (Krippendorff, 2004) treat <0.667 as tentative at best. Either soften the characterization or justify it.

5. Unsupported claims:
   - "Consistent with what several prior studies on transformer-based report generation systems have been reporting" — needs citations (e.g., Miura et al., 2021; Endo et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2023) and a more specific comparison rather than a vague gesture.
   - The attribution of weak utility agreement to "differing rating thresholds" is asserted without evidence; either show variance-component analysis or label it as a hypothesis.

6. Design concern worth addressing in text: pairwise presentation of model vs. reference report to the same rater enables direct comparison and can bias absolute Likert scores. Either justify the within-subject design or report whether a forced-choice preference was also collected.

7. Sample/population framing: 500 cases from MIMIC-CXR held-out partition — clarify whether sampling was stratified by finding type or random, and note demographic/site limitations of MIMIC-CXR that constrain generalization.

Revised text

We evaluated the chest-radiograph reports produced by our generation model using a human protocol with radiology trainees recruited through [platform name]. The study was approved by [IRB reference]. Raters provided five-point Likert judgments along three dimensions: factual accuracy, clinical utility, and fluency. Anchors for each dimension are given in Appendix [X].

Eligible raters had completed at least two years of residency. Candidates first completed a calibration task of [N] pre-rated cases and were admitted to the main pool only if their ratings fell within [tolerance] of consensus on at least [k] of [N] cases. After screening, the pool comprised 28 raters. Raters were compensated at [rate] per case.

We drew 500 frontal radiographs from the held-out partition of MIMIC-CXR [cite Johnson et al., 2019], sampled [randomly / stratified by finding category — specify]. For each radiograph we paired the model-generated report with the original radiologist report retrieved from the dataset. The two reports were presented in randomized order with no indication of source. Each pair was rated by three independent reviewers drawn from the pool; assignment was [describe scheme]. Per-pair ratings were averaged across reviewers. We computed inter-rater agreement using ordinal Krippendorff's α separately for each dimension.

On factual accuracy, model-generated reports received a mean rating of 3.8 (SD [x], 95% CI [y]) versus 4.2 ([x], [y]) for radiologist reports; the difference was [test, statistic, p]. Clinical utility means were [m_model] versus [m_ref] ([test result]). Fluency means were [m_model] versus [m_ref], with no detectable difference ([test result]). The magnitude of the factual-accuracy gap is comparable to that reported by [Miura et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2023; etc.]. Krippendorff's α was [v1] for factual accuracy, [v2] for fluency, and [v3] for clinical utility. Agreement was weakest on clinical utility; a variance-decomposition analysis (Appendix [Y]) suggests this reflects heterogeneity in raters' utility thresholds rather than item-level disagreement, though we treat this as a hypothesis rather than a finding.

Uncertainty disclosure: The specific prior-work citations I suggested (Miura et al., 2021; Endo et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2023; Johnson et al., 2019 for MIMIC-CXR) are plausible matches based on the topic but I have not verified that any of them is the most apt reference for the comparison the authors intend; the authors should confirm against their own bibliography. The Krippendorff (2004) threshold of 0.667 is from memory and should be checked against the cited edition. The bracketed numerical placeholders ([x], [y], [N], etc.) are for the authors to fill from their data; I do not have access to the actual statistics.
